Topics.Title: Introduction to Hash Compression
Topics.Summary: Defines hash compression and explains the fundamental problem of mapping large key spaces to smaller, bounded index ranges. Establishes the motivation and core vocabulary for understanding hash functions.

Elements.Title: What Is Hash Compression?
Elements.BodyText: Hash compression is the process of transforming a value from a large key space into a smaller, bounded index range suitable for use in a data structure such as a hash table.
Elements.Notes: Think of it as a funnel: many possible input values are mapped down to a limited set of output positions, making storage and retrieval efficient.

Items.Text: A hash function takes an input key and produces an integer index within a defined range.
The output range is typically determined by the size of the underlying array or table.
Compression is necessary because real-world keys (strings, large integers, objects) far outnumber the available storage slots.

Elements.Title: The Core Problem: Large Key Spaces
Elements.BodyText: The fundamental challenge in hashing is that the universe of possible keys is vastly larger than the number of available storage positions.
Elements.Notes: For example, a table with 1,000 slots must somehow accommodate keys drawn from a space of millions or even billions of possible values.

Items.Text: Key spaces can be theoretically infinite or astronomically large (e.g., all possible strings).
Storage structures have finite, predetermined capacities, creating a mismatch that hash compression must resolve.
Without compression, direct-address tables would require memory proportional to the entire key space, which is impractical.

Elements.Title: Mapping Keys to Index Ranges
Elements.BodyText: Hash compression defines a mapping from an input key to an integer index that falls within the valid bounds of a target structure.
Elements.Notes: A valid index for a table of size N must satisfy 0 ≤ index < N. The hash function's job is to guarantee this constraint for any input.

Items.Text: The mapping must produce an index within [0, N-1] for a table of size N, regardless of the input key.
The same key must always map to the same index — hash functions are deterministic.
A good mapping spreads keys as evenly as possible across the available index range to minimize clustering.

Elements.Title: Motivation for Hash Compression
Elements.BodyText: Hash compression enables fast, constant-time average lookup, insertion, and deletion by translating arbitrary keys into direct array positions.
Elements.Notes: Without an effective compression strategy, the performance benefits of hashing cannot be realized, as poor mappings lead to collisions and degraded performance.

Items.Text: Direct array access (O(1)) is only possible when a key can be reliably converted to a valid index.
Hash compression makes hash tables practical for real-world data sets with diverse and unpredictable key types.
Efficient compression is the foundation for applications such as databases, caches, and symbol tables.

Elements.Title: Core Vocabulary
Elements.BodyText: Understanding hash compression requires familiarity with a set of foundational terms that appear throughout the study of hash functions and hash tables.
Elements.Notes: Mastering this vocabulary early allows students to precisely discuss the behavior and quality of different hash compression strategies.

Items.Text: Key: the input value to be stored or retrieved, which may be any data type.
Hash function: the algorithm that converts a key into a numeric index.
Index range: the bounded set of valid output positions, typically [0, N-1].
Collision: the situation where two distinct keys map to the same index, an unavoidable consequence of compression.
Load factor: the ratio of stored entries to table capacity, influencing how often collisions occur.

Elements.Title: Properties of an Effective Hash Function
Elements.BodyText: Not all mappings from keys to indices are equally useful; a good hash function must satisfy several important properties to support efficient data access.
Elements.Notes: These properties collectively determine whether a hash function is suitable for practical use or only theoretically valid.

Items.Text: Determinism: identical inputs must always produce the same output index.
Uniform distribution: keys should be spread as evenly as possible across the index range to minimize collisions.
Efficiency: the function should compute the index quickly, ideally in O(1) or O(k) time where k is key length.
Compression guarantee: the output must always fall within the valid index range, never exceeding table bounds.

Topics.Title: Modular Arithmetic as a Compression Strategy
Topics.Summary: Explores the use of the modulo operation to compress hash values into a target index range. Examines how table size selection affects the quality and uniformity of the resulting distribution.

Elements.Title: The Role of Modular Arithmetic in Hash Compression
Elements.BodyText: Modular arithmetic provides a straightforward mechanism for compressing an arbitrary hash value into a bounded index range suitable for a hash table.
Elements.Notes: For example, if a hash value is 1,047 and the table size is 100, then 1,047 mod 100 = 47, placing the key at index 47.

Items.Text: The modulo operation (%) takes a hash value h and a table size m, returning a remainder in the range [0, m-1].
This guarantees that any hash value, regardless of magnitude, maps to a valid index within the table.
The formula index = h(k) mod m is one of the most widely used compression functions in practice.

Elements.Title: How the Modulo Operation Reduces Key Space
Elements.BodyText: The modulo operation effectively wraps a large numeric domain around a fixed-size table, collapsing an enormous key space into a manageable index range.
Elements.Notes: Think of it like a clock: no matter how many hours pass, the clock face always shows a value between 0 and 11 (mod 12).

Items.Text: Without compression, hash values could span billions of possible integers, making direct indexing impractical.
Applying mod m folds this large range into exactly m distinct slots, one for each table position.
Keys that differ greatly in magnitude can still map to the same index, which is expected behavior managed by collision resolution.

Elements.Title: Selecting Table Size: Prime Numbers and Distribution Quality
Elements.BodyText: The choice of table size m critically influences how uniformly keys are distributed across the table when using modular compression.
Elements.Notes: For instance, if m = 10 and many keys are multiples of 5, then only indices 0 and 5 will ever be used, wasting half the table.

Items.Text: Choosing a prime number for m reduces the likelihood that common patterns in key values will cause clustering at specific indices.
Non-prime table sizes can create systematic collisions when key values share a common factor with m.
Prime-sized tables help ensure that the remainder cycles through all available indices more evenly.
A well-chosen prime m that is not close to a power of 2 or 10 further avoids bias introduced by binary or decimal patterns in keys.

Elements.Title: Uniformity of Distribution and Its Importance
Elements.BodyText: A high-quality compression function spreads keys as evenly as possible across all table indices, minimizing collisions and preserving lookup efficiency.
Elements.Notes: Poor distribution causes some slots to overflow with collisions while others remain empty, degrading average-case performance toward O(n) instead of O(1).

Items.Text: Uniform distribution means each of the m slots receives approximately the same number of keys on average.
Skewed distributions arise when the compression function interacts poorly with regularities in the input key set.
Evaluating distribution quality often involves measuring the variance in slot occupancy or the load factor per bucket.

Elements.Title: Limitations and Failure Cases of Simple Modular Compression
Elements.BodyText: While easy to implement, the basic modulo method can perform poorly when input keys exhibit certain structured or repetitive patterns.
Elements.Notes: Sequential integer keys with m = 8 distribute well, but keys that are all even numbers with m = 8 will only occupy even-numbered indices.

Items.Text: Keys with a regular stride or arithmetic progression can map to only a subset of indices if that stride shares a factor with m.
Poorly chosen table sizes amplify input regularities rather than neutralizing them.
Combining modular compression with a strong preliminary hash function helps break up patterns before the mod operation is applied.

Elements.Title: Relationship Between Load Factor and Table Size Choice
Elements.BodyText: The table size m directly determines the load factor, which measures how full the table is and influences both collision frequency and performance.
Elements.Notes: A load factor above 0.7–0.8 is commonly considered a threshold for resizing the table to maintain efficient operations.

Items.Text: Load factor λ = n / m, where n is the number of stored keys and m is the table size.
A larger m reduces the load factor and collision probability but increases memory consumption.
Selecting m requires balancing memory efficiency against the performance cost of frequent collisions.
Resizing the table (rehashing) resets the load factor but requires recomputing indices for all existing keys.

Topics.Title: Folding and Other Compression Techniques
Topics.Summary: Introduces folding methods that divide keys into segments and combine them to produce a compressed index. Surveys additional compression strategies and compares their approaches to reducing key space.

Elements.Title: Introduction to Folding
Elements.BodyText: Folding is a compression technique that divides a key into multiple segments of equal or near-equal size and combines those segments to produce a smaller index value.
Elements.Notes: Folding is particularly useful when keys are large integers or long strings that exceed the desired index range.

Items.Text: The key is split into parts, typically of a fixed width matching the desired index size.
The parts are then added or otherwise combined to yield a single compressed value.
This approach distributes large key values across a smaller address space without complex arithmetic.

Elements.Title: Shift Folding
Elements.BodyText: Shift folding divides the key into segments and adds them together directly, shifting each segment into alignment before summing.
Elements.Notes: For example, the key 123456789 might be split into 123, 456, and 789, which are then summed to give 1368 as the hash base.

Items.Text: Each segment is treated as an independent numeric value and added to the others.
The resulting sum may be further reduced using modular arithmetic to fit the table size.
Shift folding is simple to implement and works well for uniformly distributed numeric keys.

Elements.Title: Boundary Folding
Elements.BodyText: Boundary folding is a variation in which alternate segments are reversed before being added, simulating the physical folding of a strip of digits at segment boundaries.
Elements.Notes: Using the same key 123456789 split into 123, 456, and 789, boundary folding would reverse every other segment, adding 123, 654, and 789 instead.

Items.Text: Reversing alternate segments introduces additional mixing, which can improve distribution over shift folding alone.
The reversal step helps avoid clustering that can occur when many keys share similar digit patterns in the same segment positions.
Boundary folding is slightly more complex to implement but often produces better spread across the index range.

Elements.Title: Digit Extraction
Elements.BodyText: Digit extraction selects specific digits or characters from predetermined positions within a key and concatenates or combines them to form the compressed index.
Elements.Notes: This technique is effective when certain positions in a key are known to vary widely while others remain nearly constant across all keys.

Items.Text: Positions with the greatest variation are chosen to maximize spread across the hash table.
Poorly chosen positions can lead to severe clustering if many keys share the same digits at those locations.
Digit extraction requires prior knowledge of the key distribution to be applied effectively.

Elements.Title: Mid-Square Method
Elements.BodyText: The mid-square method squares the key value and then extracts the middle digits of the result as the compressed index.
Elements.Notes: For a key of 3121, squaring yields 9,740,641; extracting the middle three digits might give 406 as the hash index.

Items.Text: Squaring the key causes the middle digits to depend on all digits of the original key, improving mixing.
The number of middle digits extracted is chosen to match the desired index range size.
This method can produce good distribution but requires handling very large intermediate values when keys are large.

Elements.Title: Comparing Compression Strategies
Elements.BodyText: Different compression techniques vary in computational cost, implementation complexity, and the quality of distribution they produce across the hash table.
Elements.Notes: No single method is universally best; the choice depends on the key type, key distribution, and table size requirements.

Items.Text: Folding methods are computationally inexpensive and easy to implement for numeric keys of fixed length.
Digit extraction is fast but highly dependent on domain knowledge about the key structure.
The mid-square method provides stronger mixing at the cost of handling large intermediate values.
All these techniques may be combined with modular arithmetic as a final step to ensure the index fits within the table bounds.

Topics.Title: Distribution Properties of Hash Functions
Topics.Summary: Analyzes how well different compression techniques spread keys uniformly across the index range. Discusses concepts such as clustering, load factor, and what makes a distribution desirable for hash table performance.

Elements.Title: Uniform Distribution as the Ideal Goal
Elements.BodyText: A well-designed hash function should spread keys as evenly as possible across all available index slots in the hash table.
Elements.Notes: Uniform distribution minimizes wasted space and reduces the likelihood of multiple keys competing for the same slot.

Items.Text: Uniform distribution means each index slot receives approximately the same number of keys, regardless of input patterns.
When keys cluster in certain slots, other slots remain empty, wasting memory and degrading lookup performance.
The closer a hash function approximates a uniform random distribution, the more predictable and efficient the table's performance becomes.

Elements.Title: Clustering and Its Impact on Performance
Elements.BodyText: Clustering occurs when a disproportionate number of keys hash to the same or nearby index slots, creating hotspots in the table.
Elements.Notes: Primary clustering is common with linear probing; secondary clustering can occur even with quadratic probing. Both degrade average-case lookup time.

Items.Text: Clustered keys force collision resolution to probe many occupied slots before finding an empty one, increasing average search time.
Poor compression techniques—such as a poorly chosen modulus—can cause systematic clustering if keys share common factors with the table size.
Recognizing clustering patterns helps diagnose whether a chosen compression strategy is suitable for a given key set.

Elements.Title: Load Factor and Table Occupancy
Elements.BodyText: The load factor is the ratio of the number of stored keys to the total number of slots in the hash table, and it directly influences collision frequency and performance.
Elements.Notes: A load factor of 0.7 (70% full) is often cited as a practical upper bound for open-addressing tables before performance degrades noticeably.

Items.Text: Load factor λ = n / m, where n is the number of keys inserted and m is the number of slots available.
As the load factor rises toward 1.0, the probability of collisions increases sharply, making good distribution even more critical.
Keeping the load factor low (typically below 0.75) reduces clustering and maintains near-constant average lookup time.

Elements.Title: How Compression Technique Choice Affects Distribution
Elements.BodyText: Different compression strategies—such as modular arithmetic or folding—produce different distribution qualities depending on the structure of the key set.
Elements.Notes: For example, using a modulus that shares factors with key values can cause keys to map exclusively to a subset of slots, severely reducing effective table size.

Items.Text: Modular compression with a prime-number table size tends to break up regularities in key values, improving uniformity.
Folding-based compression can distribute bit patterns more evenly but may still cluster if the folded segments are correlated.
The best compression technique for a given application depends on the expected distribution and structure of the actual keys being stored.

Elements.Title: Measuring Distribution Quality
Elements.BodyText: Evaluating how well a hash function distributes keys requires quantitative measures that go beyond simply counting collisions.
Elements.Notes: Statistical tests such as the chi-squared test can formally assess whether an observed distribution significantly deviates from uniform expectation.

Items.Text: Counting the number of collisions per slot and computing variance across slots provides a practical empirical measure of uniformity.
A low variance in slot occupancy indicates good distribution; high variance signals clustering problems.
Empirical testing with representative key samples is essential because theoretical analysis alone may not capture real-world input patterns.

Elements.Title: Desirable Properties for Hash Table Performance
Elements.BodyText: Beyond uniformity, a distribution is considered desirable when it remains stable and predictable across a wide variety of input key sets.
Elements.Notes: Hash functions used in security-sensitive applications also require unpredictability, but for general hash tables the focus is on speed and uniformity.

Items.Text: A desirable distribution minimizes both the expected number of collisions and the worst-case number of keys in any single slot.
Consistency across different key types—integers, strings, floating-point values—ensures the compression technique generalizes well.
Low computational cost combined with good distribution quality is the practical sweet spot for most hash table implementations.

Topics.Title: Comparing and Selecting Compression Methods
Topics.Summary: Provides a framework for evaluating trade-offs among compression strategies based on efficiency, uniformity, and implementation complexity. Guides students in choosing an appropriate method for a given use case.

Elements.Title: Understanding Compression Efficiency
Elements.BodyText: Efficiency in a compression method refers to how well it utilizes the available index range while minimizing wasted slots and collisions.
Elements.Notes: A method that maps most keys to only a fraction of available indices wastes space and degrades hash table performance significantly.

Items.Text: An efficient compression method distributes keys as evenly as possible across all available indices.
High collision rates indicate poor efficiency, requiring more complex collision resolution strategies and slowing lookups.
Measuring efficiency involves analyzing the load factor and the expected number of collisions under a given key distribution.

Elements.Title: Evaluating Distribution Uniformity
Elements.BodyText: Uniformity measures how evenly a compression function spreads hash values across the index range, which is critical for balanced hash table performance.
Elements.Notes: For example, modular compression using a prime table size tends to produce more uniform distributions than using a power-of-two size, which may favor lower-order bits.

Items.Text: A uniform distribution minimizes clustering, ensuring no single bucket becomes a hotspot for collisions.
The choice of table size and compression parameters directly affects uniformity; prime sizes are generally preferred for modular arithmetic.
Statistical tests such as chi-square analysis can be applied to empirically verify whether a compression method achieves near-uniform distribution.

Elements.Title: Assessing Implementation Complexity
Elements.BodyText: Implementation complexity describes how difficult it is to code, debug, and maintain a given compression method within a larger system.
Elements.Notes: Folding methods, while often producing good distributions, require more implementation steps than simple modular division, which may matter in resource-constrained environments.

Items.Text: Simple methods like the division method (mod p) are easy to implement but may require careful selection of the divisor to perform well.
More sophisticated methods such as folding or polynomial hashing offer better distribution properties but introduce additional coding and maintenance overhead.
Teams should weigh whether the performance gains of a complex method justify the added development and debugging cost.

Elements.Title: Trade-Off Framework for Method Selection
Elements.BodyText: Selecting a compression method requires balancing efficiency, uniformity, and complexity against the specific requirements and constraints of the use case.
Elements.Notes: A quick-lookup dictionary application may prioritize speed and simplicity, while a cryptographic or security-sensitive application may demand stronger distribution guarantees above all else.

Items.Text: Identify the primary performance goal first—whether it is speed, minimal collisions, or memory efficiency—before comparing methods.
Consider the key distribution of actual data; some methods perform well on random data but degrade on structured or clustered inputs.
Document the trade-offs explicitly so that future maintainers understand why a particular compression strategy was chosen.

Elements.Title: Matching Methods to Use Cases
Elements.BodyText: Different application contexts favor different compression strategies, and no single method is universally optimal across all scenarios.
Elements.Notes: For instance, folding is well-suited when keys are long numeric strings, while modular arithmetic is often sufficient for smaller, well-distributed integer key sets.

Items.Text: High-frequency, performance-critical applications benefit from computationally cheap methods like the division method with a prime modulus.
Applications with long or variable-length keys may benefit from folding, which breaks keys into segments and combines them before compression.
When key distributions are unknown or unpredictable, methods with stronger theoretical uniformity guarantees should be preferred to reduce worst-case behavior.

Elements.Title: Iterative Evaluation and Testing
Elements.BodyText: Choosing a compression method should not be a one-time decision; empirical testing with real or representative data is essential to validate theoretical expectations.
Elements.Notes: Benchmarking multiple candidate methods on actual workload data often reveals practical differences that theoretical analysis alone cannot predict.

Items.Text: Prototype candidate compression methods and measure collision rates, lookup times, and memory usage against realistic datasets.
Compare results across multiple table sizes to determine whether a method's performance is sensitive to the size parameter.
Be prepared to revisit the compression strategy if the key distribution or access patterns of the application change significantly over time.
